subject: Science
chapter: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

key_points: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures where solute particles are evenly distributed at the molecular level
Solubility depends on temperature, pressure (for gases), and the nature of the substances involved
Saturated solutions contain the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a specific temperature
Gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature because gas particles escape more easily
Density plays a major role in natural phenomena such as floating, sinking, and atmospheric movement
Changes in temperature and pressure can alter density and solubility in physical systems
examples: Nitrogen acts as the solvent in air because it is present in the largest proportion
Warm polluted water contains less oxygen, affecting aquatic organisms
Oil floats on water because it has lower density and is immiscible with water
Hot air balloons rise because heated air becomes less dense
Ice floats on water because water expands and becomes less dense when frozen
formulas_or_processes: Relative density = Density of substance / Density of water
Displacement method process: Submerge object → Measure change in water level → Calculate volume
Particle model of dissolution: Solute particles separate and spread among solvent particles
Thermal expansion: Heating causes particles to move apart increasing volume and lowering density
exam_tips: Be able to explain scientifically why objects float or sink using density comparison
Understand experimental procedures such as saturation and displacement method
Explain the effect of temperature on both solubility and density
Revise real-life applications such as ships, hot air balloons, and aquatic ecosystems
Practice explaining processes using particle-level reasoning
